observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and instruct the Chairperson of the Inventory Committee to: 1. prepare and submit to the Municipal Mayor for his approval, a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) indicating therein the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, cut-off date and schedule of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory; 2. conduct the physical inventory within the period identified in the PIP and tag all counted properties of the municipality; 3. categorize the accounted PPEs using the PPSAS account classification in the RPCPPE and prepare the report within 60 days from the completion of the physical inventory and furnish a copy of the report to the COA Team Leader, Municipal Accountant and Head of the Property Unit of the Municipality; and 4. prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and complete the disposal of the unserviceable properties. 5. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer conduct a reconciliation of the PPEs included in the RPCPPE with the recorded properties in the books of accounts of the Municipality and prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action, in cases of non-existing/missing properties, if any. 6. We recommended finally that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer, perform the procedures to account for non-existing/missing properties as a result of the reconciliation conducted to facilitate the disposition of these items in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to reflect a reliable balance of the PPE account in the Financial Statement.
observations.managementAction: Another audit observation on PPEs is discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRM Officer: 20. ensure that the PPAs in the MDRRMFIP are specific and aligned with the NDRRM Plan and that performance indicators are definite, measurable, and time-bounded; 21. implement the projects identified during the year to achieve the purpose of the fund; 22. evaluate the unimplemented programs/projects/activities and thresh out the causes of the failure to implement; and 23. revise the Plan, if necessary and see to it that PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP are responsive to the needs of the municipality.

We recommended that the General Services Officer, in coordination with the MSWD Officer, prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, its supporting documents and the registries of donated supplies and materials, contrary to COA Circular 2020-009 and 2014-002, thus, the total cost of donations received by the LGU and proper distribution thereof could not be ascertained.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the four statutory positions for the LDRRM Office to perform the functions as mandated by the regulations, subject to the PS limitation of the LGU.

We recommended that the HRMO update and reconcile with the records of all employees of the Municipality of Matag-ob on their premium contributions and status of loans to confirm any under/over-remittance of the LGU to the said agency. Also, provide the GSIS immediately with documents on changes of employees’ salaries so that GSIS billings will be accurate. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and HRMO resolve the prior years’ balances on GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations by December 31, 2020 so that all LGU Matag-ob employees can fully avail of the GSIS benefits, avoid additional loan interests and surcharges and confirm that the amount paid for the employees’ loans were not taken from LGU funds. Also, reconcile monthly their records to ensure accurate remittances.
We recommended that the Property Officer reconcile PC with the Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: Another audit observation on PPEs is discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the submission of the lacking documents to prove that the purchased lot was free from any encumbrances; the payee was the rightful owner of the purchased lot, and the sale was duly registered with the Register of Deeds.

We reiterated the recommendation that the:
a. Municipal Accountant: a) record the checks issued by the Municipal
Treasurer based on her Report of Checks Issued and the funds credited
to the LGU’s bank account based on the bank’s Credit/Debit Memos and
Bank Statements; b) reverse the disbursements covered with stale checks
and avoid its repetitive inclusion in the subsequent BRS.
b. Municipal Treasurer send written notices to the payees of the checks
which have become stale and determine whether these checks are eligible
for replacement.

In addition, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant: a) record
the reversal of entry for cancelled check identified in paragraph 1.5.6; b)
restore the unreleased checks at yearend; and c) record the identified debit
and credit memos that were left unrecorded as of December 31, 2022.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a) Municipal Accountant to (i) investigate and expedite substantiation of
noted difference between the recorded RPT Receivable and SET
Receivable; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
balances RPT Receivable and SET Receivable account duly supported
with documents; and (iii) ensure that the recorded Receivable reconcile
with the records maintained by the MTO and Assessor’s Office.
b) Municipal Treasurer to (i) Submit to the Municipal Accountant, at the
beginning of the year, a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers
and the amount due and collectible for the year based on the updated
Taxpayer’s index card; (ii) Update the Taxpayer’s Index Card, and
reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Assessor; (iii)
prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which
remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including the
reasons for such non-collection; and (iv) post a notice of the
delinquency at the main entrance of the municipal hall or in publicly
accessible places and submit such list to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB)
on or before December 31 of the year immediately succeeding the year
in which the delinquencies were incurred, and request for assistance
from the SB in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided
in RA No. 7160.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials
concerned to implement other tax collection mechanisms and strategies to
encourage delinquent taxpayers to settle their tax liabilities and increase
RPT collections of the Municipality.

We reiterated the recommendation that the:
a. The Municipal Mayor give priority in establishing a Supply Storage
Area to ensure the safekeeping of goods delivered to the Municipality,
which will greatly aid in the proper management of the Municipality's
inventories.
b. The Municipal General Services Office (MGSO), as the head of the
Inventory Committee, take the following actions: 1) initiate a physical
count of all inventory items within 30 days upon receipt of the Annual
Audit Report (AAR) for CY 2022, and prepare and submit the Report
on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant
for reconciliation purposes. Afterward, conduct a physical count of all
inventory items every end of the semester; 2) prepare the Requisition
and Issue Slip (RIS) when distributing/issuing stocks to the end-users
and consolidate all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and
Materials Issued. Submit the said report to the Municipal Accountant
as the basis for recognizing expenses in the books.
c. The Municipal Accountant should cease the practice of immediately
recording inventories received by the municipality into expense
accounts. Instead, recognize the corresponding expense account based
on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by
duly approved RIS.

We also recommended that the MGSO create and maintain updated Stock
Cards for each category of supplies with their respective stock numbers.
These Stock Cards should be reconciled with the RPCI at the end of each
year.

Moreover, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should
maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for every stock number and reconcile them
with the Stock Cards of the GSO and the RPCI of the Inventory Committee.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign a capable
regular/permanent staff to: 1) oversee the organization and monitoring of
drug, medicine, medical, and laboratory supplies inventory and records in
the RHU; and 2) enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of GSO in
performing its obligations and functions in managing and maintaining the
municipality's resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical
inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper
classification, individual identification, status of serviceability,
accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete
RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each
year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP
together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable
properties;
b. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of
recorded unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as
recommended above; (ii) make the necessary adjustments in the books to
correct erroneously classified ₱2,498,863.50 Drainage Canal at Municipal
Compound; (iii) drop from the PPE account the identified items below
the ₱15,000.00 threshold total
c. ling ₱3,840,477.22; (iv) initiate the proper turn-over of the completed
projects to the respective Barangay-Recipients supported with the
properly accomplished turn-over documents, and effect adjustments in
the books; and (v) prepare/maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for
each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and
d. direct the General Services Officer to take charge in setting up and
updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and
accountability of purchased/ acquired and issued PPEs.

We also recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of
the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular
No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are
verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer the
unexpended balance of LDRRMF to a Special Trust Fund account in the
Trust Fund books.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant generate a monthly
Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Fund (DRRMF), and ensure that any Trust Liability related to
the DRRM account is duly corrected. Further, we advise strict compliance
with COA Circular 2012-002 issued on September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. make representations with National Housing Authority and exhaust
possible remedies, either to proceed with the housing projects at the
originally proposed location/site or return the entire ₱13.050 million to
obtain a revised MOA and new funding from NHA; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain comprehensive subsidiary
records for all fund received for each project.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LGU
officials assigned as Project-in-Charge to do the following:
a. Implement the projects immediately as per the timeline specified in the
MOA to make the best use of government projects/programs and return
any unused funds, if any.
b. Prepare reports for each completed project and submit them to the
Accountant within one month of completion; and
c. Inform the Municipal Mayor about any project/program that cannot be
immediately executed for appropriate action or referral to the Source
Agency/ies.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
a.) render satisfactory explanation on certifying the correctness of the SWA
despite the noted defects and uninstalled work items/materials in the
projects and non-deduction thereof from the progress or final billings of
the contracts, without prejudice to the issuance of notice of disallowance
for deficiencies found in audit; and
b.) direct the resident/project engineers to (i) conduct thorough inspection on
all infrastructure projects for validation of the actual accomplishment
submitted for progress billing to make sure that all materials and
prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work, Detailed
Estimates and Plan or Variation Orders, if any; and (ii) prepare
Inspection Report accurately showing the actual work accomplished as
compared to the contractor’s SWA and make necessary
adjustments/deductions from the progress billing equivalent to the cost of
noted inaccuracy.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal
Agriculturist and other concerned personnel, submit justification detailing
why the constructed Slaughterhouse was left idle for a significant period of
time and specific actions/timelines they plan to undertake to ensure the
immediate utilization of the said facility.

Lastly, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss
and wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor ensure that the following measures be undertaken in the
future, for projects of similar nature:
a.) strictly monitor the proper implementation of all projects, whether
contracts for infrastructure, goods or consulting services, and strictly
monitor if they are in accord with the approved plans and specifications
within the time frame specified in the contract agreement; and
b.) identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If so,
impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions particularly
termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors for failure to
comply with their obligations, in accordance with the provisions of the
Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere with the provisions of the
Revised IRR of RA 9184, existing law, rules and regulations including
COA Circulars and latest GPPB Resolutions to ensure that all awarded
contracts be supported with sufficient and/or appropriate documents;
and
b. Municipal Engineer to require inclusion of Occupational Safety and
Health as separate pay item in every infrastructure project implemented
in compliance with DPWH D.O. No. 56 s. 2005 dated April 20, 2005 and
monitor the proper implementation of the said program to ensure its
compliance with the requirements of the DOLE and that workers are
adequately protected against occupational risks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and Municipal Accountant to:
a.) submit to the Audit Team within five (5) days, the required lacking
documents enumerated above to prove regularity and legality of the
corresponding disbursements. This is without prejudice to the issuance
of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance, if warranted;
b.) ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly supported with
complete documentation and in no case shall a disbursement be paid
without the necessary supporting documents; and
c.) strictly observe the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184,
COA Circular 2012-001 and relevant/applicable GPPB Resolutions to
ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and
public monitoring.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer should require the
contractor to post signage/billboard at the start and end of the project site
and should see to it that it is in accordance with the standard form required
under COA Circular No. 2013-004 and the Municipal Mayor should inform
the concerned SA and ATL on such posting within ten days after award of
the project as stipulated in Section 3.1 of the same circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a) Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the lacking
documents and a written explanation why the disbursement vouchers
were approved despite the deficiency; and henceforth, review the
completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers
before forwarding these to the Local Chief Executive for approval.
b) MGSO/BAC Chairman to ensure submission of complete and
appropriate documentation for Small Value Procurement required in
R.A. 9184 with completely filled-out PR, PO and AIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource
and Management Officer to:
a.) review, assess and evaluate the existing manpower, in coordination with
the department heads, to prepare an updated Organizational
Structure/Staffing Pattern Plan so that the ideal number of plantilla
personnel will be established, and submit a report with recommendations
thereon to the Local Chief Executive;
b.) formulate uniform policies and guidelines to address the issue on the
necessity and propriety of hiring JOs, taking into consideration the
existing regular employees;
c.) limit the hiring of personnel under Job Order contracts to those
extremely necessary and deploy only to offices that are actually in need of
their services;
d.) require Job Orders to submit primary personal identification, undergo
interview and evaluate the performance of each to give assurance that the
risk identified above are addressed; and
e.) incorporate in the contract the number of man hours, quantity and
duration of the work to be performed by the JO personnel and require
them to submit quantified accomplishment reports to support payments
of wages.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. issue an Acknowledgment Receipt for In-Kind Donations for DRRM or
his authorized representative for the receipt of in-kind donations.
b. designate the office to receive all in-kind donations who will:
1. establish an inventory management system on receipt, distribution
and/or utilization of in-kind donations,
2. prepare the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and
Balances every month or every quarter supported by
Acknowledgment Receipts, Proof of Receipt by and Distribution to
the Beneficiaries/ end-users, and Inventory of Remaining
Balances, and
3. submit to the Property Custodian copy of the Acknowledgment
Receipt for donated equipment and other tangible items which
costs ₱15,000 or less and has a life of more than a year.
c. require the designated Property Custodian or duly appointed GSO to:
1. maintain Stock Cards/Property Card for all donations in-kind
received and update regularly;
2. maintain Registry of Donated Relief Goods for each kind of
inventory items; and
3. prepare Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) for issuance of
donated PPEs and Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for the
issuances of tangible items which costs ₱15,000.00 or less and have
a life of more than a year and are not considered as PPE to
establish accountability over them and furnish the Municipal
Accountant with the PAR and ICS;
d. require the departments (MDRRMO, DSWDO and RHU) responsible for
the distribution of relief goods and other supplies to submit Proof of
Receipt by and Distribution to the Beneficiaries/end-users to the Audit
Team as referred to in 12.14(b).

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant summarize the data
from the documents obtained, compare records with Registry of Donated
Relief Goods maintained by the Property Custodian/GSO or by the
designated MDRRMO for check and balance, and accordingly disclose all
necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. ensure that all vehicles owned by the Municipality are promptly
registered as government vehicles with the LTO under the name of the
Municipality, include in the annual budget appropriation for the renewal
of registration payment of all vehicles, and registration be made within
the period prescribed by the LTO to avoid payment of penalties and
other sanctions; and
2. instruct the Property Custodian or Driver of the Motor Vehicle to stop
the operation/use of the vehicles with expired LTO registration.

We also recommended that the duly appointed GSO:
1. effect immediate renewal of the three (3) vehicles operating with invalid
LTO registration;
2. investigate, locate records and submit copies of registration documents
for the 13 motor vehicles;
3. keep the records of registration for all LGU vehicles, monitor their expiry
dates and schedule the annual renewal of LTO registration in
coordination with the Property Custodian or Driver of the vehicle; and
4. evaluate any unserviceable vehicles for possible disposal, and in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the disposal
procedures unserviceable vehicles, pursuant to pertinent provisions in
COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, who is responsible for
managing the finances of the municipality, should ensure that all
disbursement vouchers and their supporting documents are appropriately
stamped with the word "PAID", serially numbered, and dated.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant should ensure the
completeness and accuracy of the supporting documents attached to the
disbursement voucher, and that it is properly accomplished before
forwarding it to the LCE for approval.

Finally, we recommended that the LCE ensure strict compliance by the
officials concerned with the rules and regulations under COA Circular Nos.
92-389 and 78-79, and Volume II of NGAS Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairman of the Project Coordinators to: 11. Implement each project funded by the National Government in accordance with the time frame provided in the Memoranda of Agreement (MOA). 12. Fast-track the implementation of the delayed/unfinished programs and projects in order to utilize fully the BUB funds and ensure the proper use thereof
observations.managementAction: and 13. Ensure compliance with the provisions in the MOA and the Work and Financial Plan of the program/project. 14. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant return the unutilized balance from completed projects to the source agencies or to the Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended that the Local DRRM Officer: 18. Prepare and submit a duly certified Report on Sources and Utilization of the Funds on or before the 15th day after the end of each month, in compliance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. 19. We recommended that the MSWDO, in-charge of distribution should submit a justification on the deficiencies noted in the distribution in the disbursement vouchers for the audit team’s evaluation. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 39. prepare immediately the Fund Utilization Reports (FUR) with the complete supporting documents for all completed projects implemented out of the funds granted by National Government Agencies; 40. record all FURs immediately in the books once submitted to COA and its funding agencies; and, 41. prepare immediately the Disbursement Voucher for the return of the unutilized amount of fund transfers to the Bureau of Treasury or funding agencies as stipulated in the MOA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare journal entries to transfer the unutilized appropriation for Local DRRM Fund to the Special Trust Fund.

Moving forward, we recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer maximize the utilization of the Local DRRM Fund by implementing the     PPAs in the LDRRMF Investment Plan to strengthen the capability of the Municipality to cope with adverse effects of disasters and/or pandemic.

We recommended that the MSWDO, in-charge of distribution should submit a justification on the deficiencies noted in the distribution of relief goods for Auditor’s evaluation.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the BAC and its Secretariat submit the required supporting documents for the above-mentioned disbursements to avoid suspension of these transactions in audit otherwise, a written justification for the Audit Team’s evaluation should be submitted why payment was made on the disbursement vouchers despite the deficient supporting documents.

 For similar transactions in the future, we recommended that the BAC and    its Secretariat should comply substantially the requirement of the laws, rules and regulations applicable in the circumstances to avoid audit suspension.

We recommended that the General Services Officer, in coordination with the MSWD Officer, prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations  Received, Distributed and Balances supported with the Acknowledgment Receipts of the donations in-kind, proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries, not necessarily in the required form, and the inventory of remaining undistributed items, if any, and prepare the same for all donations received in the future, and likewise maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue a directive to the LGU officials responsible for the implementation of all projects funded by NGAs to complete its implementation in CY2020, otherwise, evaluate its viability and return the funds to the source agency if found not feasible or if the implementation cannot be undertaken by the LGU.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

prepare immediately the Fund Utilization Reports (FUR) with the complete supporting documents for all completed projects implemented out of the funds granted by National Government Agencies;

record all FURs immediately in the books once submitted to COA and its funding agencies; and,

prepare immediately the Disbursement Voucher for the return of the unutilized amount of fund transfers to the Bureau of Treasury or funding agencies as stipulated in the MOA.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 
record the checks issued by the Municipal Treasurer based on her Report of Checks Issued and the funds credited to the LGUs bank account based on the bank’s Credit Memo and Bank Statements; 

reverse the disbursements covered with stale checks and avoid its repetitive inclusion in the subsequent BRS.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: 
send written notices to the payees of the checks which have become stale and determine whether these checks are eligible for replacement; and 

request the assistance of the banks and obtain documents from them to determine the agencies which deposited the amounts to the LGU’s account.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer update the Real Property Tax Register, reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Assessor and prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one (1) year including reasons for   such   non-collection. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Assessor prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year, an updated assessment roll containing a list of real properties with reassessment values and the corresponding owners in order for the Municipal Accountant to have a correct basis in the computation of RPT Tax Receivables for the ensuing year.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer fast track the implementation of all projects in the AIP to maximize the utilization of its 20% Development Fund, especially those identified in prior years

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit justifications why Uni-Real Trading Corporation was awarded with four new contracts despite its non-disclosure in their eligibility documents.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the four statutory positions for the LDRRM Office to perform the functions as mandated by the regulations, subject to PS limitation of the LGU.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII; and,

We recommended that the HRMO update and reconcile with the GSIS the records of all employees of the Municipality of Matag-ob on their premium contributions and status of loans to confirm any under/over-remittance of the LGU to the said agency.  Also, provide the GSIS immediately with appropriate documents on changes of employees’ salaries so that GSIS billings will be accurate.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO resolve the prior years’ balances on GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations by December 31, 2020 so that all LGU-Matag-ob employees can fully avail of the GSIS benefits, avoid additional loan interests and surcharges and confirm that the amount paid for the employees’ loans were not taken from LGU funds.  Also, reconcile monthly their records to ensure accurate remittances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
trace back prior years’ transactions with Pag-IBIG Fund to resolve the negative balances of loan amortization payments of the employees to confirm that the amount paid for their loans were not taken from LGU funds;

prepare the Disbursement Voucher and its supporting documents for the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG Fund of prior years’ contributions of the employees; and,

reconcile monthly the SL against the source documents on transactions with Pag-IBIG Fund to avoid accumulation of variances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

require all Department Heads concerned to implement their respective programs and projects included in the LGU’s Annual GAD Plan and Budget duly approved by DILG; 

create the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team of the LGU in order to strengthen and maintain the effectiveness of a gender-responsive Municipality; and 

send the GAD Focal Point to GAD trainings to enhance his understanding of his role and of the program.  

We also recommended that the GAD Focal Point perform the functions called for by his designation by monitoring the programs and activities in the GPB, so that the GAD Accomplishments of the LGU at the end of the year will be compliant with regulations.
We recommended that the Property Officer reconcile PC with the subsidiary ledgers of the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the submission of the lacking documents to prove that: (a) the purchased lot was free from any encumbrances; (b) the payee was the rightful owner of the purchased lot, and; (c) the sale was duly registered with the Register of Deeds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee who will conduct the physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 and instruct the Chairperson of the Inventory Committee to:
prepare and submit to the Municipal Mayor for his approval, a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) indicating therein the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, cut-off date and schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory-taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory;
conduct the physical inventory within the period identified in the PIP and tag all counted properties of the municipality;
categorize the accounted PPEs using the PPSAS account classification in the RCPPE and prepare the report within 60 days from the completion of the physical inventory and furnish a copy of the report to the COA Team Leader, Municipal Accountant and Head of the Property Unit of the Municipality,
prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and complete the disposal of the unserviceable properties.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer conduct a reconciliation of the PPEs included in the RPCPPE with the recorded properties in the books of accounts of the Municipality and prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action, in cases of non-existing/missing properties, if any.
We recommended finally that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer, perform the procedures to account for non-existing/missing properties as a result of the reconciliation conducted to facilitate the disposition of these items in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to reflect a reliable balance of the PPE account in the Financial Statement.

We recommended that the MGSO, as head of the Inventory Committee, initiate the conduct of physical count of all inventory items within 30 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report (AAR) for CY 2021 and to prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation purposes.  Thereafter, conduct the physical count of all inventory items of the Municipality every end of the semester.
We also recommended that the MGSO prepare the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit to the Municipal Accountant as basis for the recognition of the expenses in the books.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense accounts the inventories received by the municipality. Recognize the corresponding expense account based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, prioritize the establishment of a Supply Office to ensure the safekeeping of goods delivered to the Municipality and which will greatly help in the proper management of the inventories of the municipality.

the Municipal Mayor as the Chairman of the Local Development Council (LDC) convene and reevaluate the Municipality’s Priority PPAs and its capability to implement them.
the Office of the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Office, being the implementing offices of the projects under the 20% DF, ensure that the projects identified to be implemented are procurement and implementation-ready so as to accelerate the conduct of the procurement and implementation of these projects; and

implemented and completed by the Municipality during the year
through proper scheduling and prioritization; and,
7.12.2 review the unimplemented projects in prior years funded from the
Continuing Appropriations of 20% DF during budget preparation to
determine its viability and necessity for inclusion in the AIP, otherwise,
recommend reversion of the funds if no longer needed, including the
amount appropriated for activities which is not capital in nature.

7.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer fast track the
implementation of all projects in the AIP to maximize the utilization of its 20%
Development Fund, especially those identified in prior years.

8.7 We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit
justifications why Uni-Real Trading Corporation was awarded with four new
contracts despite its non-disclosure in their eligibility documents all previously
awarded contracts.

8.8 We also recommended that the BAC scrutinize carefully the eligibility
documents submitted by interested bidders so that any misrepresentations will
be detected and to declare as eligible only those who strictly comply with the
provisions of RA 9184.

9.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and MDRRM Officer:
9.11.1 maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF by implementing the
programs, projects and activities in the LDRRMF Investment Plan;
and,
9.11.2 include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan programs, projects and
activities to be funded by the Trust Liability – DRRM which will ensure
readiness of the LGU in times of calamity.

9.12 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop using the Trust
Liability – DRRM to pay for prior years expenses unless it has been recorded as
payables, especially if the expenses are not included in the approved LDRRM Plan.

10.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the four statutory
positions for the LDRRM Office to perform the functions as mandated by the
regulations, subject to PS limitation of the LGU.

11.12 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
11.13.1 pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the
Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII;
and,
11.13.2 refrain from hiring Job Order Workers who have reached the
compulsory retirement age.

12.11 We recommended that the HRMO update and reconcile with the GSIS the
records of all employees of the Municipality of Matag-ob on their premium
contributions and status of loans to confirm any under/over-remittance of the
LGU to the said agency. Also, provide the GSIS immediately with appropriate
documents on changes of employees’ salaries so that GSIS billings will be
accurate.

12.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO resolve
the prior years’ balances on GSIS premium contributions and loan
amortizations by December 31, 2020 so that all LGU-Matag-ob employees can
84
fully avail of the GSIS benefits, avoid additional loan interests and surcharges
and confirm that the amount paid for the employees’ loans were not taken from
LGU funds. Also, reconcile monthly their records to ensure accurate
remittances.

13.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
13.9.1 trace back prior years’ transactions with Pag-IBIG Fund to resolve the
negative balances of loan amortization payments of the employees to
confirm that the amount paid for their loans were not taken from LGU
funds;
13.9.2 prepare the Disbursement Voucher and its supporting documents for
the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG Fund of prior years’
contributions of the employees; and,
13.9.3 reconcile monthly the SL against the source documents on transactions
with Pag-IBIG Fund to avoid accumulation of variances.

14.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
14.12.1 require all Department Heads concerned to implement their
respective programs and projects included in the LGU’s Annual
GAD Plan and Budget duly approved by DILG;
14.12.2 create the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team of the LGU in
order to strengthen and maintain the effectiveness of a gender-
responsive Municipality; and
14.12.3 send the GAD Focal Point to GAD trainings to enhance his
understanding of his role and of the program.

14.13 We also recommended that the GAD Focal Point perform the functions called
for by his designation by monitoring the programs and activities in the GPB,
so that the GAD Accomplishments of the LGU at the end of the year will be
compliant with regulations.

15.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, and other LGU officials
concerned comply strictly with the minimum requirements in the operation of
the LGU’s controlled dumpsite for the meantime that its sanitary landfill is
not yet operational. Also, evaluate whether the court case on the ownership
of the lot purchased by the Municipality in CY 2017 for the sanitary landfill
will be decided within CY 2020 in favor of the LGU. If not, look for another
site which has no legal impediments and start with the sanitary landfill
operation immediately.

1)We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairman of the Inventory Committee to properly conduct the physical count of LGU properties and submit a complete report thereof. All unserviceable properties should be disposed of following the procedures outlined in COA Circular 89-296. The Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee shall reconcile their reports and records to settle discrepancies and bring the balance of the PPE account in order.
observations.managementAction: No reconciliatio n was made between RPCPPE and records of the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: 7) We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LGU’s focal persons of programs and projects funded by National Government Agencies to fast-track its implementation, otherwise, return the funds to the source agency if the project cannot be implemented. 6) We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with each project focal person on the immediate submission of liquidation documents to the source agencies.
observations.managementAction: Utilization of funds received from NGAs were done in 2018.
observations.reason: Liquidation of funds transferred was still delayed

observations.recommendation: 8) We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit justifications why Qorban Construction and Supply was not declared ineligible but was awarded with five new contracts despite the entity’s non-disclosure in its eligibility documents. 9) We also recommended that the BAC and TWG review thoroughly the eligibility documents submitted by interested bidders to detect any misrepresentations and declare as eligible only those who strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Thorough review of eligibility documents was implemented by BAC.

observations.recommendation: 20)Defer disbursement of LGU funds on the lot which is not legally owned by the municipality to avoid possible wastage of funds and disallowance in audit; and; 21) Closely monitor the immediate transfer of the title to the LGU considering that advance payments has been made to the heirs, otherwise the same maybe disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has stopped disbursing funds for the said project. The subject lot was not yet transferred in the name of the LGU. Court proceedings still ongoing.

observations.recommendation: 26)We recommended that the Municipal Mayor fast track the acquisition of land for MRF and the transfer of ownership of the land at the final disposal site for the establishment of Materials Recovery Facility and Sanitary Landfill.

Likewise, require the Provincial Appraisal Committee to submit the BIR Zonal Valuation to Support the Established purchase price.
observations.managementAction: No documents were submitted by the LGU. The lot was subject to ongoing court litigation.

Reconciliation between the Inventory report and the accounting records should be conducted by the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee and for the former to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect an accurate balance of the PPE accounts. Finally, officials and employees having custody of properties which are not accounted for during the physical inventory-taking, should be made accountable.
observations.managementAction: For the year 2018, the Inventory Committee has not conducted the inventory taking of properties.

observations.managementAction: Refund was not made.

observations.recommendation: Refer to prior recommendations regarding return of unexpended PDAF to National Treasury.
